project.title: Proteomics-Based Pilot Study on Heatstroke Recovery and Organ Injury Risk Assessment: Analysis of Clinical Human Heatstroke Cases Using Plasma Samples from Mild Heatstroke Conditions
project.projectDescription: This project focuses on identifying biomarkers in non-invasive samples from clinical cases to monitor heatstroke and diagnose it early. The approach utilizes a top-down proteomics strategy applied to patient plasma samples collected at two critical time points: diagnosis and recovery. This method aims to enhance understanding and detection capabilities for better management of heatstroke.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Human plasma were collected both at the time of heatstroke diagnosis and during the recovery phase within a week. This study followed a proteomics strategy which involved sample-specific pretreatment using affinity columns to remove major proteins present in plasma samples, followed by the removal of impurities with clean-up kits. The samples were then subjected to two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2-DE) for protein separation and comprehensive image analysis of the protein profiles, followed by statistical analysis to screen for significant protein changes.  Protein spots with lFDR < 0.2 were separated from original gel and trypsin digested, following with c-tip desalting and subject to liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) for identification.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: LC-MS/MS analysis was conducted at NIMS, Tsukuba, Japan using a Thermo Scientific Q Exactive MS system and NIMS Proteome Discoverer 2.4 software. Samples, digested in-gel, were injected into a C18 column (100 µm diameter × 150 mm, 3 µm particles) and separated with a gradient of 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid in water (A) and acetonitrile (B). Chromatography operated at 35 ℃ with a flow rate of 500 nL/min over 20 minutes per sample. Electrospray ionization preceded mass measurement. Data processed via MASCOT (Matrix Science) against SwissProt 2023_01 database with trypsin digestion, specifying 350–5000 m/z for precursor ions, 0.02 Da fragment tolerance, and 10.0 PPM precursor mass tolerance. Criteria included two missed cleavage sites, carbamidomethylation of Cys residues as fixed modification, and variable Met oxidation. Identified proteins filtered to FDR 0.01, excluding keratin contaminants. Proteins with highest MASCOT scores were annotated, their molecular weight and isoelectric point matched to 2-DE images for verification and network analysis.

Characteristics[Specimen]: Fifty microliters of aliquots of human plasma were used for each experiment.
Characteristics[NumberOfSamples]: Plasma samples (n = 12) from six clinically diagnosed classic heatstroke (CHS) patients were collected at Juntendo University Hospital in 2020.
Comment[Instrument]: Benchtop Quadrupole-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Q Exactive MS system)
Comment[Separation]: C18 column (100 µm diameter × 150 mm length, packed with 3 µm C18 particles)
Comment[FlowRateChromatogram]: flow rate was set at 500 nL/min
Comment[GradientTime]: for a duration of 20 min per sample.
Comment[NumberOfMissedCleavages]: Two missed cleavage sites were allowed
